uploadedMemoTitle: Sanitized Aurora AAP-80 Public-Source Diligence Notes

oneLineAssessment.value: A plausible medium-term HALEU customer, but not yet a bankable fuel reservation counterparty until site control, licensing schedule, offtake structure, and first-core assay/specification evidence are produced.
oneLineAssessment.note: Demo synthesis only. No real LLM calls are implemented.

fuelNeed.value: HALEU metal fuel, target 12-16 metric tons for first core plus reload qualification lots

deploymentSite.value: Western U.S. industrial host site under non-binding site evaluation

readinessScores.label: Fuel Specification Maturity
readinessScores.rationale: Memo includes assay range, form factor, and first-core estimate, but lacks signed fuel design authority, qualification basis, or supplier-facing specification package.

readinessScores.label: Licensing Path Evidence
readinessScores.rationale: Public pre-application touchpoints exist, but the docket record does not yet establish a construction permit filing date or fuel qualification closure path.

readinessScores.label: Site and Customer Pull
readinessScores.rationale: A host site is described, but site control, interconnection, end-customer offtake, and state-level incentives are not evidenced in public documents.

readinessScores.label: Financeability
readinessScores.rationale: Strategic partner discussions are credible enough for diligence, but no binding customer contract, milestone financing, or cost-share award is available in the demo corpus.

readinessScores.label: Supplier Readiness Fit
readinessScores.rationale: The projected first-core timing and reload profile are attractive to a fuel-cycle supplier if validated by technical interfaces and credit support.

fuelCycleDependencies.currentEvidence: First-core HALEU quantity and assay are claimed; reservation pathway is not demonstrated.
fuelCycleDependencies.proofNeeded: Binding term sheet or board-approved reservation mandate with assay, quantity, delivery windows, and credit support.

fuelCycleDependencies.currentEvidence: No conversion routing, UF6 handling, metal fuel interface, or fallback path appears in the demo corpus.
fuelCycleDependencies.proofNeeded: Process flow, supplier shortlist, material control account plan, and interface responsibility matrix.

fuelCycleDependencies.currentEvidence: Memo names a preferred fabrication partner, but no executable agreement is provided.
fuelCycleDependencies.proofNeeded: Signed fabrication MOU or services agreement with qualification schedule, safeguards scope, and capacity allocation.

fuelCycleDependencies.currentEvidence: Public filings do not expose detailed nuclear material transport planning.
fuelCycleDependencies.proofNeeded: Confidential logistics plan, package selection, route assumptions, and responsible licensee mapping.

evidenceQuality.claim: Technology concept and intended output are defined.
evidenceQuality.diligenceImplication: Enough to classify demand type, not enough to underwrite supply timing.

evidenceQuality.claim: First-core HALEU demand is material to suppliers.
evidenceQuality.diligenceImplication: Require technical appendix and fuel design authority confirmation.

evidenceQuality.claim: Deployment site is available on target schedule.
evidenceQuality.diligenceImplication: Do not treat as near-term demand until site control evidence exists.

evidenceQuality.claim: Project can fund fuel reservation before final investment decision.
evidenceQuality.diligenceImplication: Credit and cancellation terms matter more than stated demand volume.

unknowns.unknown: Whether Aurora has budget authority to sign a fuel reservation before licensing certainty.
unknowns.whyItMatters: A supplier needs to know whether stated demand can become a binding reservation rather than an option on scarce capacity.
unknowns.requiredEvidence: Board-approved procurement authority, credit support, cancellation terms, and financing condition precedent.
unknowns.nextStep: Request board-approved procurement authority or financing condition precedent.

unknowns.unknown: Whether the claimed host site can accept nuclear construction and security envelope on the proposed schedule.
unknowns.whyItMatters: Fuel demand timing is not credible if the site, permits, security envelope, and host-customer posture are not real.
unknowns.whoShouldAnswer: Project development lead and host-site sponsor
unknowns.requiredEvidence: Site control, permitting matrix, community engagement record, interconnection status, and host-customer authorization.
unknowns.nextStep: Ask for site control, permitting matrix, community engagement record, and interconnection status.

unknowns.unknown: Final enrichment assay, chemical form, and acceptance criteria for first reload batch.
unknowns.whyItMatters: Fuel-cycle suppliers cannot underwrite production, conversion, deconversion, or fabrication interfaces from generic HALEU demand.
unknowns.whoShouldAnswer: Fuel design authority and fabrication partner
unknowns.requiredEvidence: Permissioned fuel specification package, interface control document, and qualification schedule.
unknowns.nextStep: Set up a sanitized fuel specification review with export-control screening before any confidential exchange.

unknowns.unknown: Whether alternate HALEU supply can cover delay in domestic enrichment ramp.
unknowns.whyItMatters: Supply-chain fragility can turn a credible project into an inventory or delivery-risk problem.
unknowns.whoShouldAnswer: Fuel procurement lead and supplier commercial team
unknowns.requiredEvidence: Assay-specific delay cases, substitution constraints, and public or permissioned supplier capacity assumptions.
unknowns.nextStep: Model delay cases and map substitution constraints by assay and delivery window.

counterpartyQuestions.question: What fuel reservation volume can you commit to before construction permit issuance?
counterpartyQuestions.whyItMatters: Separates real near-term demand from aspirational project schedules.
counterpartyQuestions.expectedEvidence: Procurement authority, credit support, cancellation terms, and delivery window.

counterpartyQuestions.question: Who owns fuel design authority and qualification responsibility?
counterpartyQuestions.whyItMatters: Supplier risk changes materially if the developer cannot close technical interfaces.
counterpartyQuestions.expectedEvidence: Fuel design control document, fabrication partner agreement, and qualification plan.

counterpartyQuestions.question: Which external milestones would cause you to defer first-core fuel procurement?
counterpartyQuestions.whyItMatters: Defines schedule fragility and inventory risk for suppliers.
counterpartyQuestions.expectedEvidence: Milestone dependency map tied to licensing, site, offtake, and financing.

counterpartyQuestions.question: What evidence can be shared under NDA that is not visible in public filings?
counterpartyQuestions.whyItMatters: Moves the profile from public plausibility to diligence-grade conviction.
counterpartyQuestions.expectedEvidence: Data room index, source list, and responsible executive attestation.

recommendedAction.rationale: Treat Aurora as a serious but not yet bankable HALEU prospect. The best next move is structured diligence, not a supply commitment.
conditions: No reserved production slot until fuel specification package and fuel design authority are verified.
Require site control or host-customer evidence before assigning near-term demand probability above 50%.
Offer a conditional reservation framework with credit support and staged cancellation economics.
recommendedAction.nextReviewTrigger: Re-score when Aurora provides NDA data room evidence or files a licensing milestone tied to first-core fuel.

memo.heading: Bottom line
memo.body: Aurora looks like a credible diligence target for a HALEU supplier because the claimed fuel requirement is sizable and the schedule sits inside the window where supplier capacity decisions matter. The project is not yet a confirmed future customer because the evidence stack is thin around site, finance, and fuel qualification execution.

memo.heading: What would prove deployability
memo.body: The proof package should include a controlled fuel specification, reservation authority, signed fabrication pathway, site control evidence, and a milestone-linked procurement calendar. Public sources alone cannot answer several of those questions.

memo.heading: Commercial posture
memo.body: Engage now, but make the next meeting evidence-led. A supplier should ask for data room materials before discussing priority allocation, and should price uncertainty into any conditional reservation.

sources.title: Sanitized Aurora AAP-80 project notes
sources.excerpt: Non-confidential notes claim first-core HALEU demand, target commercial operation window, preferred fabrication pathway, and non-binding host site evaluation.

sources.title: Pre-application meeting summary
sources.excerpt: Confirms public interaction around reactor concept and licensing approach, but does not validate construction schedule.

sources.title: HALEU supply market note
sources.excerpt: Describes supply scarcity, reservation behavior, and schedule risk for advanced reactor first cores.

sources.title: Regulatory readiness checklist
sources.excerpt: Lists licensing artifacts that would support a higher confidence project-readiness finding.

sources.title: Strategic partner diligence notes
sources.excerpt: Indicates partner conversations are active, but not yet converted into binding project finance or offtake evidence.

sources.title: Schedule claims reconciliation
sources.excerpt: Compares sanitized schedule claims against public evidence gaps and flags missing proof points.

claimToIcMemo.oneLineJudgment: Diligence required. Treat as potentially interesting but not yet bankable; fuel availability, licensing maturity, EPC responsibility, binding offtake evidence, and timeline compatibility remain unresolved.
claimToIcMemo.recommendedNextAction: Take the meeting only if the counterparty can provide a public-source pack or sanitized diligence room index covering fuel, licensing, offtake, EPC, and site milestones.

unsupportedClaims.claim: 300 MW behind-the-meter nuclear-powered data center campus can be delivered by 2031.
unsupportedClaims.whyItMatters: The timeline drives valuation, customer urgency, and whether near-term development capital is justified.
unsupportedClaims.requiredEvidence: Integrated schedule tied to NRC pathway, site control, EPC scope, fuel delivery, financing, and offtake milestones.

unsupportedClaims.claim: HALEU fuel will be available on the claimed schedule.
unsupportedClaims.whyItMatters: Fuel availability can become the gating item even if the reactor and customer story are credible.
unsupportedClaims.requiredEvidence: Assay-specific fuel supply plan, fabrication route, reservation authority, and contingency case.

unsupportedClaims.claim: Long-term data center offtake is real and financeable.
unsupportedClaims.whyItMatters: A non-binding customer narrative does not support project finance or capacity reservation.
unsupportedClaims.requiredEvidence: Term sheet status, credit support, pricing framework, curtailment terms, and customer approval path.

unsupportedClaims.claim: NRC engagement is advanced enough to support the 2031 date.
unsupportedClaims.whyItMatters: Regulatory maturity is the difference between a plausible development story and an investable schedule.
unsupportedClaims.requiredEvidence: Public docket record, pre-application artifacts, licensing strategy, and milestone map.

unsupportedClaims.claim: Strategic fuel-cycle and EPC partnerships will be signed soon.
unsupportedClaims.whyItMatters: Partnership expectations can mask missing execution responsibility and cost risk.
unsupportedClaims.requiredEvidence: Signed or near-final LOIs, scope matrix, risk allocation, cancellation terms, and board authorization.

timelineCredibility.title: Timeline credibility
timelineCredibility.assessment: 2031 is possible only under a very compressed path. Public evidence does not yet connect licensing, site, fuel, EPC, offtake, and financing milestones into an executable calendar.

fuelCycleReadiness.title: Fuel-cycle readiness
fuelCycleReadiness.assessment: The HALEU claim is strategically important but not bankable without assay, form, fabrication, reservation authority, and delivery-window evidence.

regulatoryMaturity.title: Regulatory / NRC status
regulatoryMaturity.assessment: Public regulatory engagement is a positive signal, but it is not equivalent to a mature licensing path capable of supporting a 2031 deployment date.

commercialOfftakeCredibility.title: Commercial / offtake credibility
commercialOfftakeCredibility.assessment: Data center offtake is the right demand narrative, but financeability depends on binding terms, credit, deliverability, pricing, and curtailment obligations that are not public.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: NRC path supports the claimed deployment date.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: Without a credible licensing path, the 2031 campus date is a commercial aspiration rather than an underwriting case.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: Public docket artifacts, licensing milestone map, safety case readiness, and regulatory engagement record.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: Fuel supply and fabrication are available on time.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: HALEU availability and fabrication capacity can gate deployment even when demand is real.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: Fuel reservation term sheet, assay-specific supply plan, fabrication partner scope, and contingency plan.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: The offtake/customer agreement is binding or financeable.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: Project finance depends on enforceable customer commitments, not stated demand for data center power.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: Signed term sheet or contract, credit support, pricing framework, and customer approval evidence.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: EPC/construction risk is wrapped or otherwise credible.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: Construction responsibility and cost overrun exposure determine whether the project can be financed.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: EPC scope matrix, contractor credentials, risk allocation, cost estimate basis, and schedule contingency.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: Site, permitting, and political risks are manageable.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: A behind-the-meter campus still needs site control, interconnection, permits, security, and local acceptance.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: Site control, permit matrix, interconnection status, local stakeholder record, and security envelope plan.

whatMustBeTrue.statement: Financing assumptions match the project's maturity.
whatMustBeTrue.whyItMatters: Capital should not be priced as infrastructure if the project is still venture-stage development risk.
whatMustBeTrue.requiredEvidence: Sources and uses, milestone financing plan, customer credit support, grants, and downside funding case.

relevantPrecedents.relevance: NRC design certification can coexist with commercial project cancellation risk.
Test reactor licensing path provides a regulatory precedent, but not direct evidence for commercial campus deployment.
DOE-backed advanced reactor demonstration highlights the importance of fuel-cycle dependencies.
Restart precedent is relevant to regulatory and financing confidence, but differs materially from new-build SMR risk.

firstPassIcMemo.target: SMR + data center power campus claim
firstPassIcMemo.situation: A developer claims it can deliver a 300 MW behind-the-meter nuclear-powered data center campus by 2031 using advanced reactors, HALEU fuel, and long-term data center offtake.
firstPassIcMemo.thesis: The opportunity is directionally aligned with data center load growth and strategic nuclear demand, but the public evidence does not yet support treating the timeline or fuel demand as bankable.
whatIsEvidenced: Public regulatory engagement can be a positive signal when tied to docket artifacts.
HALEU scarcity and advanced-reactor fuel-cycle constraints are real diligence issues.
Data center power demand creates a plausible customer pull narrative.
whatIsNotYetEvidenced: Binding fuel supply or fabrication pathway.
EPC responsibility, cost wrap, or construction schedule basis.
Site control and permitting path adequate for 2031.
NRC maturity sufficient for the claimed deployment date.
majorKillRisks: Fuel availability slips beyond the claimed commercial date.
Regulatory timeline does not support deployment by 2031.
Offtake is non-binding or not financeable.
EPC scope is not credible or exposes uncapped construction risk.
Project is priced as infrastructure despite venture-stage execution risk.
whatMustBeTrue: NRC path supports the claimed deployment date.
Fuel supply and fabrication are available on time.
The offtake/customer agreement is binding or financeable.
EPC/construction risk is wrapped or otherwise credible.
Site, permitting, and political risks are manageable.
Financing assumptions match the project's maturity.
diligenceQuestions: What public NRC artifacts support the claimed 2031 deployment path?
What HALEU assay, form, quantity, supplier, and fabrication route are assumed?
Is the data center offtake binding, exclusive, credit-supported, and financeable?
Who owns EPC scope, cost overrun risk, schedule liquidated damages, and commissioning risk?
What site control, interconnection, local permitting, and security milestones are complete?
firstPassIcMemo.recommendedNextStep: Proceed to a focused diligence call only if the company can provide a sanitized source pack and permissioned evidence index for fuel, NRC, offtake, EPC, site, and financing assumptions.
sourcesAndEvidenceNotes: Public sources can support broad regulatory and market context.
Sanitized user notes can frame claims but should not be treated as proof.
Private contracts, pricing, internal NRC feedback, and confidential MOU terms cannot be verified from public documents.
